title: Capstone: File Room Handoff
summary: Use the Linux terminal to turn a messy event workspace into a clean handoff: inspect what is there, sort files into the right folders, protect an important note with a backup, remove temporary clutter, and leave a clear ready marker.

sketchBlocks.title: Plan the handoff before changing anything
sketchBlocks.bodyMarkdown:
A good capstone starts with **inspection before action**. In a real handoff, you do not want to move or remove files until you know what is already in the workspace.

A simple inspection flow is:

```bash
pwd
ls
ls notes
```

Line by line:

- `pwd` shows your current location so you know where you are working.
- `ls` lists the items in the current folder.
- `ls notes` looks inside the `notes` folder without changing into it.

That pattern helps you avoid mistakes. If you expect event materials but only see a few files, you know to inspect more before organizing.

In this capstone, think like the person preparing a workspace for the next teammate. Your goal is not just to make changes. Your goal is to leave a workspace that is easy to understand and safe to use.

sketchBlocks.title: Build a clear structure with safe file moves
sketchBlocks.bodyMarkdown:
Once you know what is in the workspace, create a structure that matches the job. For an event handoff, folders such as `archive`, `deliverables`, and `backup` make the workspace easier to read.

Example:

```bash
mkdir archive
mkdir deliverables
mv flyer.txt deliverables/
cp notes/final-note.txt backup/final-note-copy.txt
```

Step by step:

- `mkdir archive` creates a folder for older material.
- `mkdir deliverables` creates a folder for files someone still needs.
- `mv flyer.txt deliverables/` moves a file into the correct folder.
- `cp notes/final-note.txt backup/final-note-copy.txt` keeps the original note and creates a protected copy.

Notice the difference between `mv` and `cp`:

- Use `mv` when the file should live in a new place.
- Use `cp` when you need both the original and a backup copy.

That distinction matters in a handoff because removing the only copy of an important note would be risky.

sketchBlocks.title: Finish with cleanup and proof
sketchBlocks.bodyMarkdown:
A polished handoff includes two final checks:

1. Remove clutter that should not stay.
2. Verify the finished workspace.

Example:

```bash
rm scratch.tmp
touch READY.txt
ls
ls backup
```

Line by line:

- `rm scratch.tmp` removes a temporary file that should not be handed off.
- `touch READY.txt` creates a visible marker that the workspace is ready.
- `ls` confirms the top-level structure.
- `ls backup` confirms the backup file exists where you expect it.

You can also inspect file contents when needed:

```bash
cat backup/final-note-copy.txt
```

That command helps you confirm that the backup is not just present, but also contains the note you meant to protect. In a final capstone, verification is part of the work, not an extra step.

quizDraft.title: Try: Survey the event file room
quizDraft.prompt: You are starting the event handoff. First, inspect the workspace so you know what needs organizing. Use the terminal to show your current location, list the top-level items, and list the contents of the `notes` folder. This matters because a safe cleanup starts with knowing exactly what is already there.
quizDraft.template: A file-writing code exercise should also ____ so automated tests can check it.
quizDraft.hint: Use one command to show location and `ls` commands to inspect both levels.

help.concept: This task checks basic workspace inspection. Before organizing files, use `pwd` to confirm where you are and `ls` to see both the current folder and a specific subfolder.
help.hint_1: Run `pwd`, then `ls`, then list the `notes` folder directly without moving anything yet.
help.hint_2: You need evidence of three checks: current directory, top-level contents, and the contents inside `notes`.

quizDraft.title: Try: Set up the handoff folder layout
quizDraft.prompt: Now create the folder structure for the handoff. Starting from the same event workspace, make three top-level folders named `archive`, `deliverables`, and `backup`. This matters because the next teammate should be able to tell where old material, active files, and protected copies belong at a glance.
quizDraft.template: A file-writing code exercise should also ____ so automated tests can check it.
quizDraft.hint: Use folder-creation commands only; no files need to move yet.

help.concept: This task focuses on creating a clean directory structure before sorting files. Clear folders make later move and copy commands safer and easier to understand.
help.hint_1: Create three separate folders at the top level of the workspace.
help.hint_2: You can make each folder with `mkdir`, or create all needed folders in one command if you prefer.

quizDraft.title: Try: Sort the event materials into their destinations
quizDraft.prompt: With the folders ready, organize the files. Move `flyer.txt` into `deliverables`, move `archive-old.txt` into `archive`, and copy `notes/final-note.txt` into `backup` with the new name `final-note-copy.txt`. This matters because the handoff needs both a clean layout and a protected copy of the important note.
quizDraft.template: A file-writing code exercise should also ____ so automated tests can check it.
quizDraft.hint: Use `mv` for files that should relocate and `cp` for the note that should exist in two places.

help.concept: This task checks the difference between moving and copying. `mv` relocates a file, while `cp` creates another copy so the original stays in place.
help.hint_1: Two files should leave the top level and land in named folders. The note in `notes` should stay there and also appear in `backup` under a new name.
help.hint_2: Use two `mv` commands and one `cp` command with the destination path `backup/final-note-copy.txt`.

quizDraft.title: Try: Remove temporary clutter without touching the saved note
quizDraft.prompt: The workspace is almost ready. Remove the temporary file `scratch.tmp`, but keep `checklist.txt` and the original note in `notes`. This matters because a good handoff removes throwaway clutter without deleting useful reference material.
quizDraft.template: A file-writing code exercise should also ____ so automated tests can check it.
quizDraft.hint: Use a remove command on only the temporary file named in the prompt.

help.concept: This task checks safe deletion. In terminal work, you should remove only the exact file that is temporary and leave useful files untouched.
help.hint_1: Only one top-level file should disappear in this step: `scratch.tmp`.
help.hint_2: Do not remove the `notes` folder or any note files. Target the single temporary filename directly.

quizDraft.title: Try: Leave a final ready marker and verify the handoff
quizDraft.prompt: Finish the capstone by creating a top-level file named `READY.txt`. Then verify the handoff by listing the top level and the `backup` folder. This matters because the ready marker tells the next teammate the workspace is complete, and the final checks prove the backup is present.
quizDraft.template: A file-writing code exercise should also ____ so automated tests can check it.
quizDraft.hint: Create one marker file, then use listing commands to confirm the final structure.

help.concept: This task combines final workspace marking with verification. A visible marker plus a quick listing gives the next person confidence that the handoff is complete.
help.hint_1: Create `READY.txt` at the top level, not inside another folder.
help.hint_2: After creating the marker, list the current folder and then list `backup` so the protected note copy is visible.
